intro:
Galatia was a region in central Asia Minor (modern Turkey) settled by Celtic (Galatian) tribes from Europe in the third century BC. The name is derived from the Latin form of the Greek Keltoi. In the New Testament era, Galatia referred either to the ethnic region occupied by these Celtic settlers in north-central Anatolia, or to the broader Roman province of Galatia which extended southward and included cities such as Pisidian Antioch, Iconium, Lystra, and Derbe — the cities Paul visited on his first missionary journey (Acts 16:6; Galatians 4:13).

Scholars debate whether Paul's Epistle to the Galatians addresses the northern ethnic Galatia or the southern provincial churches — the North Galatian and South Galatian theories respectively. Paul revisited the region on his second and third journeys, strengthening the churches (Acts 18:23). Peter also addresses Galatia as one of the regions of his readers (1 Peter 1:1). The churches of Galatia participated in the collection for Jerusalem saints (1 Corinthians 16:1). Galatia's Hellenistic-Celtic mix created the cultural context for the Judaizing controversy Paul addresses so forcefully in his letter to these churches.
